title: Car Fleet
description: There are n cars going to the same destination along a one-lane road. The destination is target miles away. You are given two integer arrays position and speed, both of length n, where position[i] is the position of the ith car and speed[i] is the speed of the ith car (in miles per hour). A car can never pass another car ahead of it, but it can catch up to it and drive bumper to bumper at the same speed. The faster car will slow down to match the slower car's speed. A car fleet is some non-empty set of cars driving at the same position and same speed. Note that a single car is also a car fleet. If a car catches up to a car fleet right at the destination point, it will still be considered as one fleet. Return the number of car fleets that will arrive at the destination.
constraints: All the values of position are unique
thinking_questions: A car can only catch another if it's behind it. What order should you process cars?
If car A would reach the target after car B, can A ever catch B? What happens?
Time-to-target for each car = (target-position)/speed. What does it mean when one car's time ≤ another's?

hints.L1: Process cars from closest-to-target to farthest. A car catches up to a fleet if its time-to-target is ≤ the fleet ahead of it.
hints.L2: Sort by position descending. Compute time = (target-pos)/speed for each. Use a stack: push each time; if top ≤ previous top, they merge (pop, it joins the fleet ahead).

follow_up_questions: Why does processing from closest-to-target first make sense?
What does it mean when two cars form a fleet — which one's time determines the fleet's arrival?
How does this relate to finding 'next greater element' conceptually?
solution_notes: Sort by pos descending. time=(target-pos)/speed. Stack: push if time>top, else merges into fleet ahead. O(n log n).
